item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best part of this is how realistic the historical records sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Carthagone delenda est?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a fan of history, I found this article to be really enjoyable. The idea is really fun and is explored in some nice ways. The exploration log I didn't find that engaging or interesting, except for the final audio bit, which created a brilliant atmosphere. The supplement was also perhaps my favorite part of the article, with really intriguing implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3813 was a mechanical war elephant.

Roll tide. 🏈

item.title: Amazing Work! But...
item.content:encoded:
I need to say right now this is hands down in my top 10 SCPs, it's VERY detailed, original, and masterfully written… With the exception of the exploration log. There are many times where it's not written very professionally, and sounds more like a novel description than an official document.
"Shimmy" is not an official term, I would imagine, and "The sounds of metal grinding together" Would be a good replacement for "Steel-on-steel", it's not like the Foundation to just assume things. There are many other nitpicks but they're too numerous and little to bother with. That being said, clean up the exploration log a bit and this will probably be one of the best additions to the Church lore in years.

A well deserved +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just can't with the artwork. I can understand it not looking historically accurate, but it doesnt look like any attempt was made at all to date it. The artwork is beautiful, but I think its inclusion in the article directly ruins the whole article by making me realize just how absurdly huge this scope is.

I think it should either be revised for higher saturation, or moved to a side page as an unofficial depiction.

item.title: Why All the Hate?
item.content:encoded:
I don't get all the apparent hate for this scip.

As a person with way more than a passing knowledge of the ancient Roman Empire, I absolutely loved this one.

The art didn't ruin my immersion. Hell, it fits right into the whole anomalous nature of the ancient war machine.

Where the Carthaginians got it from is likewise a non-issue. It's clearly anomalous, and barring some discovery of Hasdrubal's secret diary or what-have-you, I don't think it's unreasonable to think the Foundation would never know.

+1 guys. This was an awesome read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote Ensured™
Not that I needed any further convincing. This is amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler
– hide block

yessa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Random question: did you sneak a reference to Lord of the Rings in there? "What might do men have in the face of such unstoppable hate?" strongly reminds me of "what can men do against such reckless hate?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love great artworks and historical details. But when I finished reading felt some kind of unsatisfiedness, probably due to lack of explanation or implication about "why Carthaginian could construct it". It is apparently an anomaly. So it must be given in anomalous way. Only Bumaro's wondering is mentioned in the article.

BTW, I am finishing a translation of this. It will appear in Japanese branch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giant mechanical ancient CotBG warmachine.
60% of this concept made Pacific rim.
And 100% made 2406.
They even both submerged. But the 2406 was submerged under lake what go dry to the desert state (for real!) and make you interested in history of how it happened.
This work do not have any spark what can make it stand out.

I respectfully downvote.
I think this is the first djkaktus work which disappoint me to the downvote state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weirdly, I just sorta found myself bored with this one. Everything felt a little disjointed and all over the place. There's an elephant, there's some dry historical context, there's a doomed exploration team, there's zombies, there's a monologuing villain.

It felt like an idea in search of a theme, a justification for it's existence. There's no real *point* to the SCP that I can get at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for a greatly written story, but why isn't this a tale? this is only sort of an scp as an excuse to tell a good story…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For whatever logical/historical inconsistencies this article may have had, it was fun. It gave enough historical background for me to be sucked into it, and gave enough suspension of disbelief for me to not bat an eye at anything until I reached the comments. The art made me smile, the voice acting was fantastic, and I'm glad that we're seeing this much love being put into a single article.

+1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This was said in chat on the 4th of January, around 9-10 PM GMT. The quote I attributed was broken into two messages, one after the other and I am quoting exact wording as best as I can remember it. Adding [sic] was wrong in the context, I will remove it, I added it to make sure that the m-word was not my creation and I was quoting it directly.

Also, I'm really sorry if it came off that I was angry, quite the contrary, I am simply trying to explain myself.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This is thorough criticism but the initial thing regarding what kaktus allegedly said in Site19 (And never actually said according to multiple people, logs pending) is absolutely unnecessary.

Kaktus never said "Those motherfucking newbs, what do they know about what makes an SCP good?" [sic]". Throwing '[sic]' at the end of a quote doesn't allow you to make up a quote wholesale from how you interpret a conversation, [sic] is when theres a typo or glaring grammatical 'error' and the overall quote is left intact.

This is basically slander.

You don't like the SCP, whatever. You clearly don't. You go on at length about not liking it. Cut some slack, downvote, you've put more effort into this crit and why its not a good SCP to you than necessary.

Calm the hell down. And don't make quotes up for other members again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seriously? Just let it go. You’ve already gone out of your way to express how much you hate it.

Also, neither of those ‘quotes’ are correct. That art comment was neither my complete explanation from IRC, nor my exact words. As for the Kaktus quote- if someone said ‘motherfucking newbs’ it would have been logged on O5 as a Disciplinary note. Yet I can’t find anything of the sort. I know that Kaktus was angry in chat the other day, but from what I have heard from several trustworthy people, it wasn’t vehemently targeted at newbies and it didn’t include insults as blatant as this. You even tried to admit the falsity of the quote with [sic] but really, something this inflammatory isn’t to be take lightly and you can’t just make it up as you like. Taking an instance where someone is upset and changing their words to give a false impression of them is honestly appalling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry if this is against the rules here, but I'm making a second post since the first one I made was related to another conversation that people were having. I wanted to make a more comprehensive post typing my own thoughts on this SCP.

+ In reply to Djkaktus
- In reply to Djkaktus


Soon after I made my previous post which spun off a conversation about art style, Djkaktus came into the #site19 IRC chat and said "Those motherfucking newbs, what do they know about what makes an SCP good?" (presumably in regards to the conversation taking part under my post). Now, I know that kaktus was just blowing off steam, which is why I didn't take any offense to it, however I thought it was important to address this before I start.

I am no writer, unfortunately I don't have a creative bone in my body, I am however, an avid reader. I have no idea what makes an SCP good, if you were to ask me to put it into words, I would be left stumped. However, I have read almost 2500 SCPs and tales over the course of the last 6 years and I think this has developed a healthy sense of 'good' and 'bad' in regards to the quality of an SCP and left me in a favorable position to provide some (possibly valuable) feedback.

+ Concept
- Concept

It's a simple concept, a behemoth, mechanical, war elephant. The idea. In my mind, it's similar enough to Kaktus' own 001 proposal (giant mechanical behemoth minus the consuming stuff around it part, powered by anomalous means related to the broken god), while still being distinct in that it was controlled and used for earthly purposes (i.e. the conquest of Rome).

I love the very idea of a giant mechanical war elephant (who wouldn't?), I love the idea that the thing had an obvious weakness (i.e. the legs), and the Romans were smart enough to exploit it. I love the idea that the Carthaginians were lulled into a false sense of security by the elephant, and I love that the romans were smart in selectively avoiding it.

I hate the idea that the thing was forgotten about and that the removal of the collective memory of its existance isn't expanded upon. I hate that the relation to the broken god and how the technology to build this thing was obtained and then consequently lost, isn't expanded upon.

+ Style of Writing
- Style of Writing

I have read Djkaktus's SCPs before and they usually have absolutely captivating writing, he masterfully paints portraits on the mind of the reader, forcing them to visualize what is written. Reading this scp however, I felt none of that. This may have been due to the additional help garnered from actual visualizations (more on those later) or due to the fact that this might have had multiple authors (I have no idea how the work on this was divided) or it may simply be on Kaktus himself. I do not know. Again, I am no writer, I can't tell you exactly what is wrong here, all I can say is that if I read 2399 and I read 3813, if the wiki didn't tell me both were by the same author, it wouldn't have occurred to me. The writing in 3813 seems much more casual, unlike the more 'robotic' styles that I've come to expect out of SCP articles. The writing is more close to that I would expect from a tale.

I'm sure other writers would be able to give more constructive critisism on this point, and as such I'm going to leave it at that.

+ Artwork
- Artwork

Many people have already commented on the time period that the artwork is supposed to be from and I'm not going to comment on it again.

In a conversation I had with Zhange on the IRC, it was said that: 'The artwork's age and coloration could be explained in many ways, the artist may have had access to much more expensive paints, or he may have had a unique style, many people have unique styles'

And I was convinced, the stylistic deviations may be explained by countless many things, and as such talking about it was moot. I just thought I'd include this quote since it was not posted by any of the authors or artists on my previous post asking for clarification on the art.

+ Audio Log
- Audio Log

The audio log was probably the best portion of this article, I wish it was longer and continued for the full length of the initial exploration, from when the MTF agents leave the submersible all the way to when they're caught and killed. However it is ok as it is.

It may be better if the audio log transcript was collapsed, but that's just nit picking.

At the end of the day, after reading it twice, I stand by a no vote. It was a good concept, not a very enjoyable read.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Agreed. MonsterGroup, keep the critique constructive without the sarcastic snark, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels a little unnecessarily hostile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy another episode of MTF getting horribly murdered while the villain monologues.

You've presented a great Idea and presented in the most awful, mediocre bog standard way. Those MTFs should hvae gotten back in the sub the moment their cameras went out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…told ya we needed to drop every goddamn thing we have on those Carthaginian fucks. Backstabbing elephant-riding sons of bitches.

+1

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Everyone has their sticking points. In this case, the anachronism in the paintings taxes my suspension of disbelief far more then the giant mechanical war elephant (which, honestly, is pretty normal for the site). The war elephant is anomalous by definition, but the style of the paintings just is.

Saying the paintings were re-creations/variations on the theme of lost originals by later (say, Renaissance) artists, for example, would be far more plausible, as that's an extremely common occurrence in art history. That said, I don't really expect the authors to change anything on behalf of the small minority who care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is one of those reluctant downvotes. I want to like it, but I feel like the article wasn't a satisfying read. The concept (ancient Broken God war mech in a real historical setting) is fun. The voice acting is good, really good, actually! The art isn't realistic, but it's still the high quality you expect out of Zhange, and my suspension of disbelief can easily take the hit. The problem is, I just don't find it that entertaining. The historical bits in the main article are probably my favorite part. They're well written, engaging, and incredibly atmospheric. Unfortunately, the supplement log drags on, and even feels boring at times. The bit about the modern CotBG is also interesting, but that ultimately goes nowhere. Aside from that, there's the exploration log. I don't like MTF TPKs, but I'm willing to overlook those if the log is good otherwise. Unfortunately, I don't think the log is good. I feel like the descriptions of the interior could be more interesting, or provide some surprise beyond the clockwork gear and semi-tangible brass zombies. Ultimately, I feel like the meh and bad outweigh the good on this one.

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Due to concerns raised, addressing Zhange here.

This last comment is excessively rude and dismissive, and reflects poorly on both the SCP community and staff.

Everyone needs to refrain from making disparaging personal remarks towards others, staff especially so. Furthermore, it does no one any good to completely dismiss the worth of anyone's opinion simply because they don't have material on the site yet. Going forward, please refrain from this sort of behavior.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Why are you linking a film clip out of nowhere? If I'm remembering the film right, the movie is about a mad scientist. This being made by a mad scientist has an in-universe explanation, even if it's cartoony.

Secondly, why this film out of all things?

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NHRtlXDOqOU

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying you have to be 100% accurate all the time when it comes to fiction. That would be ridiculous. But accuracy needs to there to keep the audience at least somewhat grounded in reality. If someone made a Civil War film but with WW2 era tanks you'd have every right to criticize it for being accurate when it has no in universe justification.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded: Except no. If we stuck to fully historically accurate then we'd never get to tell the story of that one time roman legions successfully fought off a giant mechanical elephant. And that's not a world I wanna live in.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't talking about the gunfight I was talking about the gun. If the gun itself was thrusted into the audience's face and had a big big deal made out of it, then yes, you'd have every right to hate the movie for it not being accurate. Misread that, nvm.

Also, while I personally haven't seen the film and would probably agree that it's entertaining, I think you'd have every right to dislike the movie for it's inaccuracies. Historical media should be criticized for being inaccurate. (I'm guilty for making inaccurate historical media as well, if that counts for anything.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
I was just using one example.

The gunfight at the center of that film is also quite inaccurate. And also highly entertaining.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Saying that the paintings ruins the experience somehow is like saying if I watch Tombstone and Doc Holliday is using a gun that didn't exist at the time it's a bad movie.

The gun isn't thrusted into the audience's face in the film. Plus, knowing a gun wasn't developed until a couple years from when the film was meant to take place isn't that large of a time frame. A couple of decades in terms of technology isn't that big of a deal (at least to most people) to be noticeable unless you're interested in that field to begin with. A jump in hundreds of years of artistic style that I think was only developed into the renaissance era is noticeable, even if you aren't a person who pays alot of attention to Roman art.

The art is great. I just don't think it belongs here.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
For the record, my personal level of verisimilitude was fine with the artwork. There's enough visual records of extremely dubious quality in terms of suspension of disbelief on the wiki that something somewhat anachronistic in terms of style doesn't really bother me.

At some point, you accept that you're reading a story about a gigantic magical war-mecha elephant attacking Rome, and you're either on board with it, or you're not. Fixating on this particular issue seems a little silly to me.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
I don't know that this is something that needs to be fixed. Saying that the paintings ruins the experience somehow is like saying if I watch Tombstone and Doc Holliday is using a gun that didn't exist at the time it's a bad movie.

When we consume fiction there are certain acceptable breaks from reality that we tolerate in the pursuit of ease of narrative. I'm not saying your solution is bad, in fact I quite like it… but it doesn't have to be in the article to work as a justification either.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Alright. I propose this be fixed thusly:

Add in here a simple footnote which mentions the general style and medium are atypical of the time period; though compositional analysis is able to verify its supposed origin.

(But Shaggy! That's just hand waving!)

Yes, voices in my head. That's why we commit to that shit with good, old fashioned, SCPWorldbuildngTM!

See, then someone writes a Tale featuring Zhaites and how he becomes one of the first anartists to tap into The Flow.

And everyone wins.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded: You're right, the Romans used Vermilion for red, which was made from a rock ore. For some reason I was thinking of Cochineal which are insects that are farmed for their red pigmentation.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Red was the most abundant, made from blood of various animals,

Err, source? Blood dries brown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3813 was a mechanical war elephant.

Honestly, I'm a man of simple tastes and damn near hit the upvote button right there. Finished the skip and the supplement, did not change my position. Sure, there's probably some historical wrinkles, but this is cool enough that I can overlook them.

Excellent work, all of y'all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like one of the main problems here for me is the opposite; scope. Yeah, the scope is huge, and there's nothing bad about that. But you can only have too many large-scope SCPs before the world gets too crowded

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Not from my point of view, as the art style itself is the most jarring anachronism; looking at examples of Roman landscapes such as this and this, I can't suspend my disbelief enough to see the art in this article as from the same period and culture.

I obviously don't expect Zhange to redo all the art just because I got worked up about it, though, so I don't have anything else to say on the matter.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Colors as recent as the Renaissance Era were extremely expensive and the Romans would have access to some pallets of red, yellows and very rarely, blue. Blue coloration would have to be made with Lapis Lazuli (a rare rock found in Afghanistan). Red was the most abundant, made from blood of various animals, yellows were also made from organic materials.

The pictures could make more sense if they were described as restorations. However then you'd have to get into art styles and the history of art perspective which I am grossly under-qualified to do. But like I said, the most realistic thing would be for them to not be paintings at all, but rather sculptures, or even paintings of sculptures which you could explain as recreations.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
From what I got reading the document, the hostile mobile humanoids are deluded that they are still stopping people fleeing Rome via the ocean. Another thing I got was they were only attacking people getting close to the elephant, like really close, the type of close you need a submersible to achieve.

While its true that it has the potential to be a Keter, there is nothing I got from this article which says Keter to me. The area it's supposedly in is a common fishing destination. Most modern fishing boats have SPR (surface penetrating radars) which should be able to see this thing, in my head, the foundation would want to take a more proactive approach to containing this thing rather than just letting it sit and waiting for someone to find, then mind wiping them.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it would be more believable for these to be modern restorations to the centuries-old artworks? I don't know a whole ton of the efficacy of such restorations in restoring the original coloring and whatnot, but would that be more palatable for those who are taking issue with this perceived anachronism?

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
I definitely feel this is keter.

Between mobile humanoids who are hostile, a giant anomaly like the rotating gear, and the overall ease of discovery, this is definitely keter.

This thing could at any time, start disgorging CotBG zombies in to the sea, and do a lot of damage.

That's pretty keter to me.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
I understand that, however, remaining in universe, what is preventing the foundation from cordoning off a small portion of the ocean? It's a 500x500 meter square of the ocean 17km away from rome, which would put it at a depth of less than 1000 meters <source>. Has the foundation not contained harder to contain anomalies?

Edit: Source is the wrong sea, but the depth is similar and point still stands.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Whether something attacks is directly relevant to how difficult it is to contain though?

Anyway, I don't buy the 'it's Keter because someone could easily find it' explanation. After 2000 years, being buried in the seabed and with some creative camouflage, it should be relatively (for the Foundation) easily covered up as 'dangerous shipwreck stay away'. Hell, even if it still obviously looked like an elephant you could tell people it was some dumped movie prop.

Re again: the art being out of place, it's not quite right to say that using a painting is implausible in itself - there are surviving examples of Roman painting. However, they are all wall frescoes and woodblock portraits, and I don't think a rich Roman would have wanted this painted on his house. BoronBiggs is right that surviving parchment/vellum art would be super rare (perhaps nonexistent? Not sure about this) and in Roman 'painting' this was only used for illuminated manuscripts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After much mulling it over, have decided to upvote. While I still have my reservations about the exploration log, there's just too much brilliant stuff here to not upvote.

item.title: Re: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Object classification has nothing to do with how dangerous it is, so whether or not it attacks is irrelevant. It's entirely based on how difficult it is to contain, and an enormous metal elephant that they are completely unable to move in shallow water off the coast of one of the major tourist cities of the world sounds pretty difficult to contain to me.
Keter makes perfect sense.

item.title: Artwork
item.content:encoded:
Since other people have commented, I thought I'd expand, the artwork does seems extremely out of place. This is mostly because this whole thing is thought to be taking place somewhere around 1st century BC, for comparison, here's some actual artwork from the 1st century BC. Notice how almost none of it is paintings, that's because paintings, especially those done on leather or vellum (the materials which would have been available to artists at the time) don't survive for 2000 years, especially not if they've not been kept in extremely careful preservation. Most ancient artwork is sculpture, pottery and engravings on stone, or metals like brass and bronze.

While I really appreciate Zhange's work, the context it is presented under is far from realistic. The most realistic depiction would have been a sculpture (Romans were big on those) or an inscription.

Beyond that, I have some questions as to why exactly is it classed as Keter? I really don't buy Kaktus' reddit explanation either. It presumably doesn't attack unless you dive into the water to get close to it and you're telling me that the foundation doesn't think it can effectively cordon off a small, shallow, part of the ocean? Somehow I don't buy that.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am intrigued as to how this elephant was designed. Most of the descriptions suggest it standing on land or in (relatively) shallow water, which is consistent with the catapults and ballistae on its back. But that means Adarr of Syracuse is using considerable poetic licence (writing well after the fact?) when he suggests that it is moving from Palermo "towards Rome" - the average depth of the Tyrrhenian Sea is 2000m, meaning it is more likely that the elephant would be hugging the coastline and heading due east across the top of Sicily.

The other option is that the elephant was always designed to be fully submersible, which is perhaps suggested by the descriptions of valves etc in the exploration logs, but nowhere else. It also seems a complex design feature that would have no military purpose and limited benefits in transport - I'm not sure it would be quicker walking up and down undersea mountains than just going around the coast (and how would the accompanying fleet track the elephant's progress?).

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
This discussion has crossed the line into personal attacks, so I'm shutting this down. No further replies in this sub-thread are to be made by anyone.

This is a closed Staff Post. Do not reply to this unless you're staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You, Volgun and Zhange are all extremely well known in the SCP community for one thing or another and I find it likely that someone would upvote this irregardless of quality simply due to the fact of You all being a part of it (of which is probably not your intention).

Honestly? Even if DrGears, Djoric and Twistedgears, authors I very much love, made something and it sucked balls, I would downvote it. I upvote/downvote based on the quality of the article, even if the authors are very well known.

item.title: lol
item.content:encoded: When you actually make something of worth instead of insulting the works of people who genuinely care about SCP, maybe your opinion would be of some value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not saying you shouldn't or that this is an upvote grab.
Just that I can see how someone else would see it that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then well known artists shouldn't ever work together? Because non-contributors will see that as upvote grabbing? Give me a break.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TBH, I don't really like the way you're taking this format screw, kaktus. I don't feel like mobile is rendering it properly, and it honestly just seems excessive.

That, combined with the fact that I never really felt hooked by this, led to my novote. This isn't not well written; hell, it just might be the fact that I'm functioning on 4 hours of sleep right now. It just never appealed to me.

E: Now that I'm well rested, I'm going to have to maintain my novote. Though you do have an interesting concept, this reminded me a bit too much of your 001 for me to enjoy it. In addition, the art, though well done, did take me out of the immersion. This is definitely well written, and I can appreciate why others liked it, which is why I didn't downvote.

collapsed because this got kind of rant-y
– hide block

I was pondering why I didn't like this skip. It's definitely well-written, and it provides a compelling story. But eventually I realized that this shares a common problem with several newer Series 3 and Series 4 skips; the scope is too large.

I hate to compare newer skips to S1 skips, but I have to here. When we moved from S1 to the newer series, we gained a lot of things, but in the process, we also lost a couple. One of the things I miss dearly are small, believeable skips.

I remember when I first discovered SCP, I was pouring over the wiki, finding things like the stairwell and the shy guy and the tape of Ronald Reagan. Though I did like them for other reasons, I also liked them for their believability. These things could have actually existed in some government bunker somewhere. It's like that feeling you get when you read Harry Potter and you think that your Hogwarts ticket is just around the corner. It gives you a sense of wonder.

I feel like this is why I liked Gears' and qntm's proposals so much. They're small in scope, yet they manage to convey a believable and compelling story. It also why I like SCP-2000. It's massive in scope, yet it pulls it off while making it all believable enough that if you walked into some abandoned shed in Yellowstone, you might actually find yourself in a secret science bunker (though I appreciate it more for its extremely high quality of writing and technobabble).

Then enter Series 3 and 4. We get things like the sun satellites, the Jupiter destroyer, the space lobster and the light-speed probes. These things are absolutely MASSIVE in scope, and they pretty much crush all believability for the reader. If I looked outside my window with a telescope, I could confirm that there really isn't a solid blob of black matter orbiting Earth. This also applies to all of the skips where the Foundation is given cartoonishly advanced technology. This just really takes out the believability for me.

This is also why I feel like a lot of site members here are getting "meta fatigue." By nature, these skips literally jump out at you and remind you that you're reading something fictional.

I feel like scope and believability aren't mutually exclusive. Kaktus has done it with SCP-2935 and Famine has done it with SCP-2682. These skips are brutally large in scope, yet they barely tread over my believability, and leave me with a sense of wonder/terror.

By all means, I'm not saying unbelieveable skips are bad. I've read skips that completely destroyed my sense of belief, but still left me satisfied with a good story and great writing. Should we go back to the old, thing-that-does-a-thing stages of Series I? Hell no. We're in a much better place now than we were then. I really just want to point out this increasing problem of spectacle creep that we're facing in the Foundation nowadays.

God, this got long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this kind of multimedia collaboration is awesome, and something I'd love to see more of. I do feel like the concept of the article is overshadowed by its execution, but the sheer amount of talent converging here is awesome to see. More importantly, it does look like something friends enjoyed making together, and that's inspiring - it makes me excited for the potential of this format and the chance to be part of a community where I, too, can try cool stuff like this with cool people.

On another note, sincere congratulations to Zhange and the Volgun for achieving enough prominence to be subjects of insinuations like the ones made upthread! When you're a tall poppy, people come to cut you down; being accused of having such an impressive reputation that you can use it to bring attention to an article is, IMO, a milestone you should both be proud of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand why someone would call this an upvote grab (Don't necessarily agree with but can see why). You, Volgun and Zhange are all extremely well known in the SCP community for one thing or another and I find it likely that someone would upvote this irregardless of quality simply due to the fact of You all being a part of it (of which is probably not your intention).

The fact that their stuff (especially zhange's) clashes with the article may also make it seem forced-in.
